docs.title: Home
docs.text:
This is the root of our AP world docs.

This is mostly extra documentation and things we've found out the hard way for the apworlds we've worked on.

You're best served by making sure you're reading the latest version, which should always be here: https://useless-allure.github.io/apdocs/latest/

So far the games there are some extra docs here for are:

  • Spyro the Dragon (PS1)

docs.title: Latest AP World
docs.text:
Right now, the latest hotfix version is avaialable in the Archipelago discord here: https://discord.com/channels/731205301247803413/1281652479779536958/1508309230438973641

The Archipelago discord itself is linked from the top right corner of the Archipelago homepage: https://archipelago.gg/

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
The game info page is in gamefreq0's repository here: https://github.com/gamefreq0/Archipelago/blob/spyro/worlds/spyro/docs/en_Spyro%20the%20Dragon.md

The setup docs are in gamefreq0's repository here: https://github.com/gamefreq0/Archipelago/blob/spyro/worlds/spyro/docs/guide_en.md

docs.title: Optional Poptracker
docs.text:
Leaddybug has designed a boss MacGuffin and Goal tracker, available here: https://github.com/LeaddyBug/Spyro-1-Boss-MacGuffin-and-Goal-Tracker

Why isn't there more to the poptracker?

The codebase is undergoing an overhaul since August of 2025. I am actually the person responsible for that overhaul. I apologize. Anyway, a good poptracker is awaiting some resources I can give with less effort once the overhaul is done. I wanted to lower the labor threshold for poptracker creators in general.

docs.title: Game Version
docs.text:
Must be Spyro the Dragon (USA) for the Playstation 1.

Checksum the .bin file. (Replace spyro.bin with the name of your file in the following directions.)

In Windows 10/11, this is done via certutil using a command prompt:

Batchfile
certutil -hashfile spyro.bin MD5

In most Linux distros, this is done via md5sum:

Bash
md5sum spyro.bin

The expected sum is:

Text Only
387185f71cd0043fd75a19521dc75104

You can learn more about this on the redump page for that disc if it's relevant to you: http://redump.org/disc/576/. This is also a place to grab a cue sheet if you're missing one (a .cue file with the bin).

We do not support any other checksum for that .bin file at this time.

Europe? Australia?

I want to do this and gamefreq0 isn't opposed. However, the first goal is to get the formats for everything stable and USA running again first.

docs.title: PS1 Bios
docs.text: The only viable bios seems to be a USA PS1 bios from the original hardware. It's not impossible that any for the actual PS1 would work if it can boot the game, but we know for sure that the PS3 version of the PS1 bios does not work. We believe this has to do with how the bios initializes RAM and some data that the bizhawk connector itself needs.

docs.title: Emulator Core
docs.text:
Gamefreq0 is a linux developer. He only has the Nymashock core available for development and testing. You are best off using Nymashock to play this APWorld.

Duckstation?

This is a long and storied history, but the simplest version is that Duckstation is hostile to Linux packaging, so it's unreasonable to expect Gamefreq0 to support it. Actually; Gamefreq0 and I sat and talked about this at some point. We aren't opposed to Duckstation support, but we've no intention of doing it ourselves. In theory, the underlying libs I've been working on should make this easier, but I don't plan to write a Duckstation implementation for them, either.

This isn't the only thing Duckstation did. However, the leader of that team is pretty... spirited... when people have criticisms about it all. I think he'll agree it's fair to say he's had it with Linux users and it's reasonable that Linux users would see that and decide not to engage with it.

docs.title: The Need for a Blog
docs.text:
greysondn - The need for a blog

It's increasingly apparent I can't remember what I'm doing half the time. We needed a blog both to record decisions we've made and to document things that are ad hoc until we implement them properly. That's the whole gist of it.

docs.title: Config settings as items
docs.text:
greysondn - Config settings as items

I had this notion I ran away with that we could do config settings as items so they'd be easier to work with. But gamefreq0 pointed out that there was a possibility we were loading a gun to shoot ourselves in the foot with it. Some disagreement ensued, but we did eventually figure it out.

Things that cannot be represented as items:

  • Things which have distinct, complex values
    • In the case of Spyro
      • Spyro's overlay color (if/when that is implemented) - RGBA
      • global_gem_percent - UINT
      • max_level_gem_threshold - UINT
  • Anything which represents mutually exlusive states
    • goal settings usually go here
    • starting world goes here
    • in the case of Spyro, "no lives" versus "exploit: die to get to homeworld" (if we end up doing that)
  • Anything which should only meaningfully affect generation
    • specific goal settings
    • entrance randomization
    • most randomization settings being "on"/etc

Things that can (and maybe should!) be represented as items:

  • Speciific glitches the player is opting into the usage of
    • only ones chosen "correctly" in config apply to world generation.
    • This lets the player adjust on the fly via !getitem/etc and provides a basis for checking what the player has opted into for checking accessibility during generation/poptracker/etc. The only problem is that there's no easy way to opt-out once the player has opted-in.

gamefreq0 - Approach to an opt-out

My thinking is that there is an option to opt-out, without requiring using get_logical_path for every single location it might apply to: comparing slot_data to opt-in glitch items, and in the case of mismatch, show any in logic with later items as glitched logic in Universal Tracker; if a player opted into any glitches they were mistaken about, those glitches will show as in logic, but highlighted in yellow, as opposed to showing up like every other logically accessible location that was true at generation time.

Even if we accept (as greysondn notes below) that this is a deliberate choice, it seems it would be preferable to mark the locations with a different logic connotation from the world's generation, in order to distinguish in-logic locations from out-of-logic locations.

greysondn - About the opt-out

This feels like a mistake the player likely only makes once. The notion proposed in voice discussion about this passage was an example of a glitch opt-in item that is ambiguously named. The real issue is this: neither myself nor gamefreq0 wish to set the player up to fail even once (well, outside the rules of the game, anyway). So we have to resolve this in order to keep all the nice things this gives us without hurting players who "don't know better" yet.

  • Moves the player has access to (even when move randomization is off)
    • This lets us check location accessibility in a uniform way, for all gameplay options.

greysondn - Realm of possibility?

During voice discussion of this, gamefreq0 raised that the AP generator may go a bit crazy about items which exist but cannot or are not placed. My thinking is that we could always make a "connected" location, but it seems to me it's only an issue if our location to item ratio is a mess.

docs.title: Callbacks
docs.text:
There was discourse previously (six months ago?) on callbacks for (at minimum) items and locations. That discourse is mostly lost - we have no idea where it went. We have some notes; we need to work out our callbacks again:

  • items
    • pre_receive
    • on_receive
    • post_receive
    • on_hint
  • locations
    • pre_send
    • on_send
    • post_send
    • on_hint
    • on_scout
    • on_collect
    • on_release

There are some documents regarding the structure of these. They seem to only list what is necessary for items, however; that is, they're incomplete.

All of these default to type nop. Defining and registering a function is poorly defined and will likely end up defined ad-hoc as I figure out how I (greysondn) wish to do so.

Callbacks of type...

  • nop - do nothing, default
  • set_memory - label on tin
  • increment_memory - label on tin
  • decrement_memory - label on tin

Similar to guards, type-param-value. Unlike guards, it's a list of callbacks, not necessarily just one.

On this page
  • 20260712
    • Move Randomization Prerequisites
    • World Map Redesign

docs.title: Move Randomization Prerequisites
docs.text:
greysondn - Re: Move randomization

One of gamefreq0's goals is move randomization. That requires us to have certain things done.

  • Issues
    • Player can end up in a world where they cannot get to the balloonist.
      • Examples
        • Dream Weavers without glide
        • Magic Crafters without charge
        • Beast Makers without jump (if we even do that one)
      • Solution
        • Archipelago assumes that you can return to starting possition
        • Player just starts a new game to get back to starting position
      • QOL outlay
        • We need to init the collectibles so the player is in the exact same state in a newgame.
        • Requires knowing where all the gems are in memory.
          • Literally means gemsanity will probably happen before move randomization.

docs.title: World Map Redesign
docs.text:
greysondn - Re: World Map

Prior world map was complicated by excess connections. A minimal case is simpler and requires much less custom code. This is that map.

flowchart TD
    world["world"];
    balloonist["balloonist"];

    start_hub["Start Hub (dynamic)"];
    hub_2["Hub 2"];
    hub_3["Hub 3"];
    hub_4["Hub 4"];
    hub_5["Hub 5"];
    gnasty["Gnasty's World"];

    levels_st["levels"];
    levels_2["levels"];
    levels_3["levels"];
    levels_4["levels"];
    levels_5["levels"];
    levels_gnasty["levels"];

    final_level["final level"]
    goal["goal"]

    world --> start_hub;

    start_hub --> balloonist;
    balloonist --> start_hub;
    balloonist --> hub_2;
    balloonist --> hub_3;
    balloonist --> hub_4;
    balloonist --> hub_5;
    balloonist -->gnasty;

    start_hub --> levels_st;
    hub_2 --> levels_2;
    hub_3 --> levels_3;
    hub_4 --> levels_4;
    hub_5 --> levels_5;
    gnasty --> levels_gnasty;

    gnasty --> final_level;
    final_level--> goal;

docs.title: Model Chat Display Test
docs.text:
greysondn - Re: Model Chat Display

A quick and messy plugin, this. I need to test it, though. So. Feel free to play around with this if you really want to, I guess.

This plugin exists in anticipation of more complete AI disclosures at user requests in the discord channel. The anticipated plan is to just dump the whole chat for users to examine, with PII stripped.

Commentary

This is an example of a system message.

claude2025-06-01T14:30:00-04:00

Claude didn't say this, it's just an example of a model message.

This is an example of another system message.

greysondn2025-06-01T14:31:12-04:00Thoughts

hopefully this works well.

That should be enough for a basic test, I think.

greysondn

Eh, it works. Awful but functional for a test.

docs.title: Todo List for rewrite-1 branch
docs.text:
greysondn - my tasklist

This is just a view of what I can see. It may fall out of date. It may shorten and/or expand.

** Last known update: 13 July 2026 **

  • Data files
    • Memory addresses
      • Design
      • Implementation
        • data/memory/
    • Basic version of regions
      • Design
      • Implementation
        • data/regions/

      • Design
      • Implementation
        • this is part of data/regions/
    • Basic version of items
      • Design
      • Implementation
        • data/items/
    • Basic version of connections
      • Design
      • Implementation
        • this is part of data/regions/
    • lightweight file to use in identifying Spyro version
      • Design
      • Implementation
  • APpetite
    • Parse and represent data clauses
      • meta
      • entrance_rando
      • items
      • memory
      • regions
      • patches
      • common/important subclauses
        • sources
        • guard
        • callback
    • full internal representation
    • compat client interface base for AP
    • release APpetite 0.1
  • Feature parity, release, and modernization
    • Proper archipelago client built over APpetite
    • Implemenration of dyamic region connections
      • world to starting hub
      • starting hub to balloonist
    • restore portal sanity
    • dangling updates to become main development branch
    • push into gamefreq0's repo
    • release α1
    • update to current AP standards
    • release α2
  • Post-release
    • APpetite
      • Data exporters
        • Poptracker
        • Memory map

docs.title: Callbacks List
docs.text:
greysondn - Callbacks

I just need a good view of what all the callbacks are as we decide/identify them, nothing complex here.

docs.title: Hook points
docs.text:
Hook points take the name format of [time]_[verb].

  • items
    • pre_receive
    • on_receive
    • post_receive
    • on_hint
  • locations
    • pre_obtain
    • on_obtain
    • post_obtain
    • on_collect - called when a player collects the location
    • on_scout
    • on_hint
  • connections
    • on_connect - called during generation when the connection is actually connected.

docs.title: Callbacks
docs.text:
Actual callbacks, per object.

  • nop - do nothing (default)
  • write_memory - label on tin
  • increment_memory - label on tin
  • decrement_memory - label on tin
  • ensure_memory - label on tin

docs.title: Guards List
docs.text:
greysondn - Ad Hoc Development

I've been developing this very, very ad-hoc, so I need a list of what I've decided on the fly.

YAML
-
    type: always
-
    type: and
    params:
        next: [list[GUARD]]
-
    type: has_item
    params:
         name: [str: ITEM NAME]
         count: [uint]
-
    type: memory_equals
    params:
        name: [str: MEMORY NAME]
        value: [any: LEGAL VALUE]
-
    type: never
-
    type: not
    params:
        next: [GUARD]
-
    type: or
    params:
        next: [list[GUARD]]

docs.title: Expansion to Entrance Rando
docs.text:
greysondn - Entrance Rando

Right now, entrance rando is typically 1:1. But what we're seeing with Spyro is more like 1:1:1, which gamefreq0 represented by splitting it up 1:1 + 1:1 and then doing custom logic over those.

It feels like this is a generalizable problem. I've not quite pieced together the exact data here, however, so... it has to wait. It's just an observation right now.

12 July 2026. Can be found in the 1-1 chat with gamefreq0 by searching for 45c77609-b04e-4f6e-9d13-119bd118eb16 if you have access (all two people in the world).
